ROBOT: A programmable mechanical machine that can perform automated physical tasks.
LOGIC: The strict, step-by-step rules a computer follows to make decisions (If/Then).
MOUSE: A hand-controlled hardware input device used to move a cursor on a monitor.
STACK: A data structure where elements are layered on top of each other (Last In, First Out).
PIXEL: The tiny individual square of colored light that builds up a digital screen image.
ARRAY: An ordered list of items stored side-by-side inside your computer code.
MODEM: A peripheral device that connects your local network infrastructure to the internet.
CLOUD: Remote computer servers across the world used to store your files and host web apps.
CACHE: Super fast temporary storage space used to hold data so apps can load quicker.
INPUT: Any command, file, or keypress data you feed into a computer program from outside.
BUILD: Compiling raw human-readable source code into an executable application layer.
INDEX: The exact location address of an item inside an array (computers count from 0!).
CYBER: Anything relating to digital networks, information technology, or security layers.
PHONE: A compact mobile computer optimized for wireless networks and communication.
CLICK: The action of pressing down on a mouse switch to execute an interactive logic macro.
PRINT: A command that instructs software to textually output data to the user console.
DRIVE: Physical storage hardware medium where your operational system files live.
TOUCH: Interacting with a screen layer using the minor electrical field of your fingertips.
SMART: Devices linked together on a network to run automated background logic tasks.
CABLE: Copper wires or optical fibers bundled together to carry data signals between electronics.
